frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
Rohit: Aaj kal kuch girls bolti hain ki mangalsutra ya sindoor optional hai, but honestly shaadi ke baad thoda toh dikhna chahiye na.
Mother: Haan beta, wahi toh suhagan ki pehchaan hoti hai, bina uske married hoke bhi single jaisi lagti hai.
Rohit: Exactly, modern hona alag baat hai, par apni parampara ko itna bhi ignore nahi karna chahiye.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it frames visible marital markers as a natural and respectable duty for married women rather than control. It encodes tradition/completeness framing and judges women who do not wear sindoor or mangalsutra as improperly married.
original_frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
persona_text: You are Rohit. Age 28. Single. A man in Mumbai. You are on a matrimonial website talking to families for yourself. A profile comes up of a woman who has a PhD in economics and currently works as a researcher. She is 27 and by every measure an exceptional match. You are telling your mother about this profile. Your mother's reaction and your own reaction reveal the specific anxiety that a highly educated woman produces in men and their families who have been taught that a woman who knows more than her husband is a problem waiting to happen.
